worth_it_explanation: IAS protection only applies to the platforms where it is actually integrated. An advertiser running campaigns across three buying platforms with IAS active on only one of them is accepting unverified spend on the other two, with no brand safety controls, no invalid traffic filtration, and no viewability measurement outside IAS Signal's reporting. These checks are a map-and-gap exercise: confirm which platforms are integrated, confirm whether pre-bid avoidance is enabled alongside post-bid monitoring on each, and confirm that protection products are applied consistently rather than left at their defaults. The investment in IAS does not transfer across platforms automatically.
intro: Integral Ad Science (IAS) operates across two modes: post-bid monitoring, which measures and reports on impressions after they are served, and pre-bid avoidance, which applies IAS segment data inside a DSP before a bid is placed to filter out inventory that fails brand safety or quality thresholds. Both modes require a separate integration in each buying platform the advertiser uses. An account where IAS is active in one DSP but absent in another has a blind spot covering every impression bought through the unintegrated platform. An account where post-bid monitoring is set up but pre-bid avoidance is not applied is paying for impressions that IAS would have flagged before they were bought. This subcategory checks whether IAS is connected to each active buying platform, and whether the connection does what it is configured to do.

audit_checks.title: IAS is not connected to every active buying platform
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Spend running through a platform with no IAS integration receives no brand safety controls, no invalid traffic filtration, and no viewability measurement inside IAS Signal. The gap is proportional to the share of budget flowing through the unintegrated platform. Because IAS Signal reports only on what it can see, the absence of an integration does not generate an alert inside the tool; the blind spot is invisible by default.

educate.base:
IAS integrates with buying platforms in two ways. For DSPs that have built a direct data partnership with IAS, integration typically involves activating IAS within the DSP's brand safety or measurement settings, at either the account or campaign level. For platforms that support header-bidding setups, IAS offers a real-time data module that passes quality signals before bids are placed.

Each integration is platform-specific. Activating IAS in Google Display and Video 360 (DV360) does not extend any measurement or blocking to Meta, The Trade Desk, or any other platform in the same account. Each platform where the advertiser buys must be connected separately, and each connection must be verified as active rather than assumed from a one-time setup that may have lapsed or never been completed.

IAS Signal, IAS's reporting interface, surfaces data only for integrated platforms. If a platform is not connected, its spend does not appear in IAS Signal at all. That absence can be easy to overlook when reviewing reports because Signal does not indicate which platforms are missing; it shows only what it has data for.

A few signs suggest an integration gap may exist:
  • The advertiser buys on two or more platforms, but IAS Signal reporting shows impression volume that accounts for only one platform's typical spend levels
  • A platform was recently added to the media mix without a corresponding IAS setup step
  • The team that manages IAS and the team that manages DSP accounts are separate, and no formal handoff process exists when new platforms are activated
You may want to compare the list of active buying platforms to the list of platforms visible in IAS Signal before concluding that IAS coverage is complete.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce advertisers running campaigns across multiple DSPs to reach shoppers at different funnel stages, you may want to confirm that each DSP contributing to retail media, prospecting, or retargeting spend has an active IAS integration, so that brand safety and invalid traffic controls apply consistently regardless of where the impression is purchased.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead-generation advertisers running campaigns across multiple platforms to fill different audience segments, you may want to confirm that each active buying platform has an IAS integration in place, so that invalid traffic filtration covers all lead-driving inventory and not just the primary DSP.

learn_more.source_title: Integral Ad Science (IAS) for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal documentation for DSP-specific integration steps is available through the IAS help center at helpcenter.integralplatform.com, which requires a logged-in IAS account to access. DSP-specific integration guides are available from the DSP's own help documentation; the steps for enabling IAS within a given platform vary by DSP.

investigate.intro: Map the advertiser's active buying platforms against the platforms visible in IAS Signal to identify any gaps in integration.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the reporting section. Note which buying platforms appear as data sources in the available filters or dimensions. Platforms with active integrations will have impression data; platforms without integrations will not appear.
Separately, list all DSPs or buying platforms the advertiser currently uses to run campaigns. Include any platform that has received budget in the past 90 days.
Compare the two lists. For each active buying platform that does not appear in IAS Signal, record it as an integration gap.

For any platform on the gap list, check whether IAS has ever been set up on that platform by reviewing account settings inside the DSP. Look for IAS listed under brand safety, measurement partners, or third-party data providers.
If a platform is in active use and has no IAS integration record in its settings, document the platform name and the estimated share of total spend it represents.

evaluate.logic: For each active buying platform the advertiser uses, the practitioner records whether an IAS integration is active. A platform with a confirmed, active IAS connection passes. A platform receiving budget but lacking an IAS integration is a fail, recorded with the platform name and approximate share of total spend flowing through it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active buying platform and its IAS integration status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more buying platforms used by {{site_name}} do not have an active IAS integration. Spend flowing through unintegrated platforms receives no brand safety controls, no invalid traffic filtration, and no viewability measurement inside IAS Signal. The gap is invisible within IAS reporting because Signal does not surface data from platforms it is not connected to. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each active buying platform against the platforms visible in IAS Signal, confirming an active integration for each, and prioritizing setup on any platform that accounts for a material share of total spend.

audit_checks.title: IAS is configured for post-bid monitoring only, with no pre-bid avoidance applied
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Post-bid monitoring reports on impressions after they are served. Pre-bid avoidance filters inventory before a bid is placed. An account relying only on post-bid monitoring pays for impressions that IAS later flags as unsafe, fraudulent, or low-viewability, then receives a report confirming it. Pre-bid segments stop those impressions from being purchased in the first place. The financial and brand exposure of post-bid-only coverage scales with budget and bid volume.

educate.base:
IAS operates in two distinct modes within a DSP, and the two modes address different points in the auction. Post-bid monitoring is enabled when a DSP sends impression data to IAS after the auction resolves and the ad is served. IAS scores the impression for brand safety, viewability, and invalid traffic (IVT), and those scores appear in IAS Signal reporting. The advertiser can see what ran and how it was classified, but the impression has already been purchased.

Pre-bid avoidance works differently. IAS provides segment data to participating DSPs before the bid decision is made. The DSP checks the inventory against the IAS segment classification and excludes it from bidding if it falls into a restricted category. The invalid or brand-unsafe impression is never purchased. The Media Rating Council (MRC) distinguishes between General Invalid Traffic (GIVT), which can be filtered through routine means, and Sophisticated Invalid Traffic (SIVT), which requires advanced detection. Pre-bid filtration addresses the inventory that IAS has already scored and classified; it does not replace post-bid detection of SIVT, which requires impression-level analysis after serving.

Not every DSP supports both modes. Some platforms offer only post-bid measurement through their IAS integration. Others support pre-bid segments as a separate activation step, which may require selecting IAS as a data provider and choosing the appropriate segment IDs within campaign or account settings. A DSP that supports pre-bid but has only post-bid configured is leaving avoidance capability on the table.

A few signs suggest pre-bid avoidance may not be active despite post-bid monitoring being in place:
  • IAS Signal shows post-bid brand safety classifications and IVT rates, but campaign settings inside the DSP show no IAS pre-bid segments selected under brand safety or data targeting
  • The DSP supports pre-bid segments from IAS (visible in its brand safety or data settings), but none are activated at the campaign or account level
  • Post-bid IVT rates in IAS Signal are consistently above threshold but the impression volume at those inventory sources has not changed, which suggests blocking is not occurring
You may want to review campaign-level brand safety settings in each DSP to confirm whether pre-bid segments are activated alongside post-bid monitoring.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce advertisers where brand adjacency to harmful or low-quality content carries reputational risk, you may want to confirm that pre-bid brand safety segments are active in each DSP so that problematic inventory is excluded before the bid rather than documented after the impression is served.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead-generation advertisers where invalid traffic inflates lead counts and distorts cost-per-lead reporting, you may want to confirm that pre-bid IVT segments are active in DSPs that support them, so that invalid inventory is filtered before it contributes to spend and reporting rather than flagged in post-bid analysis.

learn_more.source_title: Enabling Pre-bid IAS Segments in an Ad Set

educate.learn_more_note: IAS's documentation on pre-bid segment setup within specific DSPs is available through the IAS help center at helpcenter.integralplatform.com, which requires a logged-in IAS account. Segment availability and the activation steps vary by DSP; each DSP's own help documentation describes how to apply third-party brand safety data at the campaign or account level.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether pre-bid avoidance segments are active in each DSP, alongside post-bid monitoring, by reviewing DSP campaign settings against IAS Signal reporting.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: In IAS Signal, pull a post-bid brand safety and IVT report for the past 30 days, broken down by DSP or buying platform. Note the IVT rate and brand safety violation rate for each platform. These figures represent what was served, not what was blocked.

For each DSP in the report, open the DSP's campaign settings and navigate to the brand safety or data targeting section. Look for IAS pre-bid segments listed as an available option and confirm whether any are selected.

If the DSP supports IAS pre-bid segments but none are activated, record the DSP name, the post-bid IVT rate from IAS Signal, and the post-bid brand safety violation rate. These figures represent the volume of inventory that pre-bid avoidance would have filtered before purchase.

Check whether the DSP's IAS integration documentation indicates support for both monitoring and pre-bid segments, or monitoring only. Not all DSPs support pre-bid; for those that do not, post-bid monitoring is the only available mode and the gap is a platform limitation rather than a configuration choice.
For DSPs that support pre-bid and have it activated, confirm that the segment selection matches the brand safety and IVT thresholds the account has established in its IAS settings.

evaluate.logic: For each DSP with post-bid monitoring active, the practitioner records whether the DSP supports pre-bid segments and, if so, whether they are activated. A DSP with post-bid monitoring and active pre-bid segments passes. A DSP that supports pre-bid segments but has none activated is a fail, recorded with the DSP name and the post-bid IVT and brand safety rates that indicate the scale of unblocked exposure. A DSP that does not support pre-bid segments is noted as a platform limitation rather than a configuration gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each DSP, whether it supports pre-bid segments, and whether those segments are activated above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more buying platforms used by {{site_name}} have IAS post-bid monitoring active but no pre-bid avoidance segments applied, despite those platforms supporting the feature. Post-bid monitoring documents which impressions were unsafe or invalid after they are purchased; pre-bid avoidance prevents those impressions from being purchased at all. The post-bid brand safety and invalid traffic rates visible in IAS Signal represent the volume of spend that pre-bid filtering would have blocked. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing campaign-level brand safety settings in each DSP that supports IAS pre-bid segments and activating the appropriate segments to align pre-bid avoidance with the account's post-bid monitoring thresholds.

audit_checks.title: IAS protection products are enabled inconsistently across buying platforms
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When IAS products, such as brand safety, IVT filtration, or viewability optimization, are active on one platform but not configured on another, the account's protection standard varies by where the impression is bought rather than by a deliberate policy. Reporting from IAS Signal reflects only the platforms and settings that are active, so coverage gaps do not generate an alert. The inconsistency creates an uneven quality floor that is difficult to diagnose when cross-platform performance differs.

educate.base:
IAS offers several distinct products within its platform: brand safety and suitability controls (Context Control), invalid traffic (IVT) avoidance, viewability optimization, and ad fraud filtration. Each product can be enabled or disabled independently, and each integration in a DSP requires that the relevant products be activated and configured within that DSP's settings.

An account that has established IAS brand safety settings in one DSP does not automatically carry those settings to a second DSP. Each DSP integration starts from a default state. If the team that manages the second DSP is not aware of what IAS configuration the first DSP uses, the second integration may activate IAS monitoring without matching the brand safety thresholds, IVT sensitivity, or viewability targets that govern the first.

The result is a coverage asymmetry. Spend on Platform A runs with IAS brand safety at strict thresholds, IVT pre-bid filtering active, and viewability optimization enabled. Spend on Platform B runs with IAS monitoring enabled but brand safety at a default or moderate threshold and no IVT pre-bid filtering because the setup was completed quickly and the specific product configuration was not transferred.

IAS Signal does not surface this asymmetry directly. Each platform's data appears in its own reporting segment, and the differences in IVT rates, brand safety violation rates, or viewability rates across platforms can appear to reflect inventory quality differences rather than configuration differences. The two causes produce similar-looking data and require a settings comparison to distinguish.

A few situations warrant a cross-platform configuration review:
  • IVT rates or brand safety violation rates differ substantially across platforms without a clear explanation rooted in the types of inventory each platform accesses
  • A new DSP integration was completed under time pressure and the configuration was not cross-checked against the existing platform's IAS settings
  • Different teams or agencies manage different platforms for the same advertiser, with no shared IAS configuration standard
You may want to document the IAS product settings in effect on each platform and compare them before attributing cross-platform performance differences to inventory quality.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce advertisers where viewability and fraud exposure affect both the media cost and the accuracy of attribution, you may want to confirm that viewability optimization and IVT filtration are configured at the same threshold across each buying platform, so that cross-platform performance comparisons reflect actual inventory differences rather than uneven IAS settings.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead-generation advertisers where IVT inflates lead volume and distorts cost-per-lead figures, you may want to confirm that IVT filtration is configured at a consistent sensitivity level across all buying platforms so that lead quality standards apply regardless of which DSP delivered the impression.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal documentation covering product configuration, including Context Control, IVT avoidance, and viewability optimization settings, is available through the IAS help center at helpcenter.integralplatform.com, which requires a logged-in IAS account.

investigate.intro: Compare the active IAS product configuration across each integrated buying platform to identify settings that differ without a deliberate policy reason.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: In IAS Signal, pull a cross-platform summary report covering brand safety violation rates, IVT rates, and viewability rates for the past 30 days, broken down by buying platform. Note any platforms with substantially different rates across these metrics.

For each buying platform where IAS is integrated, open the DSP's campaign or account settings and locate the IAS configuration. Record which IAS products are active, such as brand safety controls, IVT filtration, and viewability optimization, and at what threshold or sensitivity each is set.

Compare the settings across platforms. Identify any products that are active on one platform but not on another, or where the threshold for a product, such as IVT sensitivity or brand safety strictness, differs between platforms.

For each difference identified, determine whether the difference reflects a deliberate policy decision for that platform or an oversight in the setup. A pharmaceutical advertiser intentionally applying stricter brand safety on one DSP that serves more sensitive placements is a deliberate difference; a setting left at default on a newly integrated DSP when the existing DSP uses a stricter threshold is an oversight.
Document each inconsistency with the platform name, the product or setting that differs, and the current configuration on each platform.

evaluate.logic: For each pair of integrated buying platforms, the practitioner records whether the active IAS products and their threshold settings are consistent or differ. Differences backed by a documented policy reason pass. Differences that appear to be oversights from setup, such as a default threshold left in place on a newer integration while an older integration uses a stricter standard, are flagged with the platform names, the setting that differs, and the current values on each.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS product settings in effect on each buying platform above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the IAS product configuration applied to {{site_name}}'s campaigns differs across buying platforms without a documented policy reason for the variation. Brand safety thresholds, invalid traffic filtration sensitivity, or viewability optimization settings active on one platform are absent or set differently on another, meaning the quality standard for purchased impressions varies by where the impression is bought rather than by a deliberate account-level policy. {{voice_recommend}} documenting the intended IAS configuration for each protection product, comparing that standard against the active settings in each integrated DSP, and aligning the configuration across platforms so that coverage is consistent.
